title: Windows
subtitle: Like virtual desktops, scoped to one tmux session
summary: Windows are full-screen layouts of panes. Switch with n/p or 0–9. Rename with comma. Close with &.

blocks.text: A tmux window is a full-screen layout of one or more panes. Think of it as a virtual desktop: you can have many windows in the same session, each containing different panes. Only one window is visible at a time; the others wait, with their shells running, until you switch to them.

rows: Cycle preset pane layouts in current window

blocks.text: Rename your windows. {key:Ctrl-B}{key:,} lets you give a window a meaningful name — *editor*, *server*, *logs* — and the name shows up in the status bar. After 5+ windows the numbers stop being memorable; names always work.

There's no rule, but a common pattern is one window per *task*: an editor window with code, a server window running the dev server, a logs window tailing whatever's relevant, a scratch window for ad-hoc shell work. When you switch tasks, switch windows. When the task is done, kill the window. The session sticks around.

blocks.title: Windows vs. Panes — When to Use Which
blocks.text:
Panes and windows do similar things — they both hold shells — but they answer different questions:

  • A **pane** is for things you want to *see at the same time*. Editor + tests, code + diff, command + log tail. The screen is divided.
  • A **window** is for things you only want to see *one at a time*. A different task, a different project, a different context.

A good rule of thumb: if you find yourself zooming a pane ({key:Ctrl-B}{key:z}) all the time because the others are too cramped, those should probably be a window each. If you find yourself flipping ({key:Ctrl-B}{key:n}) between windows constantly to compare two outputs, those should probably be panes side by side.
